title: Federated Learning
description: FedAvg, secure aggregation, differential privacy in FL, communication efficiency, heterogeneous FL, systems challenges, and cross-device vs cross-silo settings.
details: Federated Learning trains models across decentralized devices or servers without centralizing raw data. This file covers the canonical FedAvg algorithm, cross-device vs cross-silo settings (millions of mobile phones vs handful of hospitals), communication efficiency (gradient compression, quantization, sparsification, FedAvg communication rounds analysis), statistical heterogeneity (non-IID data across clients — the biggest practical challenge), systems heterogeneity (different device capabilities, stragglers, dropouts), privacy guarantees via differential privacy (DP-FedAvg, RDP accountant, clipping, noise calibration) and secure aggregation (SecAgg via secret sharing, SecAgg+ efficient verification), personalized FL (local fine-tuning, multi-task learning, meta-learning per client), and practical FL benchmarks (LEAF, TensorFlow Federated, PySyft, Flower). Federated learning appears in ML system design and privacy-focused ML interviews.

sections.title: FedAvg & Communication Efficiency
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Instead of sending raw data to a central server, each client trains locally and sends only model updates (gradients or weights). The server averages these updates. This preserves privacy (data never leaves the device) and reduces bandwidth (only weights, not data, are transmitted).

FedAvg algorithm:

  • Round t: Server sends current global model wt to a random subset of clients St.
  • Client k: Run E epochs of local SGD on wt using local data Dk. Send update wt+1k back to server.
  • Server: Aggregate: wt+1 = Σ (nk / n) · wt+1k (weighted by dataset size nk).
  • Key hyperparameters: E (local epochs), fraction C of clients per round, local batch size B.
  • Convergence: FedAvg converges for convex problems (O(1/T) for strongly convex). For non-convex (neural nets), convergence to stationary point is O(1/√T) under bounded heterogeneity.

Communication efficiency:

  • FedAvg already reduces communication by having E > 1 (more local work between rounds). Optimal tradeoff: more local computation reduces communication rounds but increases model drift (clients diverge from global optimum).
  • Gradient compression: (1) Quantization: compress 32-bit floats to 1–8 bits. (2) Sparsification: send only top-k% gradients by magnitude (set rest to zero). (3) Random rotation: apply random orthogonal matrix before quantizing (improves quantization by spreading information evenly).
  • Differential privacy: For formal privacy guarantees, add Gaussian noise to each client's update: Δwk + N(0, σ²). Clip update to bound sensitivity: Δwk / max(1, ||Δwk|| / S).
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np
from copy import deepcopy

# === FedAvg on synthetic data ===
class FederatedClient:
    def __init__(self, model, data, client_id):
        self.model = deepcopy(model)
        self.data = data  # (X, y) local to this client
        self.id = client_id
        self.n = len(data[0]) if data else 0
    
    def local_train(self, global_weights, epochs=5, lr=0.01, batch_size=32):
        """Train on local data starting from global weights."""
        self.model.load_state_dict(global_weights)
        self.model.train()
        
        X, y = self.data
        optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(self.model.parameters(), lr=lr)
        
        for _ in range(epochs):
            # Mini-batch SGD
            idx = np.random.permutation(len(X))
            for i in range(0, len(X), batch_size):
                batch_idx = idx[i:i + batch_size]
                x_batch = torch.tensor(X[batch_idx], dtype=torch.float32)
                y_batch = torch.tensor(y[batch_idx], dtype=torch.long)
                
                optimizer.zero_grad()
                loss = F.cross_entropy(self.model(x_batch), y_batch)
                loss.backward()
                optimizer.step()
        
        return deepcopy(self.model.state_dict()), self.n

class FedAvgServer:
    def __init__(self, model):
        self.global_model = model
        self.global_weights = model.state_dict()
    
    def aggregate(self, client_updates, client_sizes):
        """Weighted average of client models."""
        total_size = sum(client_sizes)
        
        new_weights = deepcopy(self.global_weights)
        for key in new_weights:
            new_weights[key] = sum(
                (sz / total_size) * updates[key]
                for sz, updates in zip(client_sizes, client_updates)
            )
        
        self.global_weights = new_weights
        self.global_model.load_state_dict(new_weights)
        return new_weights

# === Full FedAvg round ===
def fedavg_round(server, clients, fraction=0.3):
    """Run one FedAvg round on a random client subset."""
    n = len(clients)
    m = max(1, int(n * fraction))
    selected = np.random.choice(clients, m, replace=False)
    
    updates = []
    sizes = []
    
    for client in selected:
        new_weights, n_samples = client.local_train(
            server.global_weights, epochs=5
        )
        updates.append(new_weights)
        sizes.append(n_samples)
    
    server.aggregate(updates, sizes)
    return server.global_model

# === DP-FedAvg (add noise + clip) ===
class DPFedAvgServer(FedAvgServer):
    def __init__(self, model, noise_scale=0.01, clip_norm=1.0):
        super().__init__(model)
        self.noise_scale = noise_scale
        self.clip_norm = clip_norm
    
    def aggregate(self, client_updates, client_sizes):
        total_size = sum(client_sizes)
        new_weights = deepcopy(self.global_weights)
        
        for key in new_weights:
            # Clip each client update
            clipped = []
            for updates in client_updates:
                delta = updates[key] - self.global_weights[key]
                norm = delta.norm()
                if norm > self.clip_norm:
                    delta = delta * self.clip_norm / norm
                clipped.append(delta)
            
            # Average + Gaussian noise
            avg = sum((sz / total_size) * clip for sz, clip in zip(client_sizes, clipped))
            avg += self.noise_scale * torch.randn_like(avg)
            new_weights[key] = self.global_weights[key] + avg
        
        self.global_weights = new_weights
        self.global_model.load_state_dict(new_weights)

# === Secure Aggregation (simplified: pairwise masks) ===
# Each client generates a random mask M_ij shared with client j
# Mask cancels out when masks are summed across clients
# Only the sum of updates is revealed to server, not individual updates

sections.title: Heterogeneity: Statistical & Systems
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: In practice, client data is not IID (each user's phone has different photos, typing patterns, etc.) and clients have different capabilities (battery, network, compute). Heterogeneity is the #1 challenge in real-world FL deployments.

Statistical heterogeneity (non-IID data):

  • Label distribution skew: Each client has different class proportions (e.g., user A only types in English, user B only types in Spanish). Most common non-IID pattern.
  • Feature distribution skew: Same label, different features across clients (e.g., different handwriting styles).
  • Quantity skew: Different clients have vastly different amounts of data.
  • Consequences: Client drift (local models diverge from global optimum). Global model may not converge, may underperform for all clients.

Solutions for statistical heterogeneity:

  • FedProx: Add proximal term to local loss: Llocal + (μ/2) ||w - wt||². Prevents local model from diverging too far from global model. Allows variable local epochs without drifting.
  • SCAFFOLD (Stochastic Controlled Averaging): Use control variates to correct client drift. Server maintains a global control variate c. Each client computes local correction ck - c. Corrected update: gk - ck + c. Provably removes the effect of data heterogeneity (linear speedup even with non-IID data).
  • Personalized FL: Instead of one global model, learn per-client models. Approaches: local fine-tuning (train global, fine-tune on each client), multi-task learning (each client is a separate task), meta-learning (MAML for fast client adaptation).

Systems heterogeneity: Clients have different compute, network, and battery. Stragglers (slow clients) delay the entire round. Solutions: asynchronous FL, client dropout tolerance, partial participation, tiered FL (group clients by capability).
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# === FedProx: proximal term to prevent drift ===
def fedprox_loss(model, global_model, mu=0.01):
    """Add proximal penalty to local loss."""
    proximal = 0
    for param, global_param in zip(model.parameters(),
                                     global_model.parameters()):
        proximal += (param - global_param).norm(2) ** 2
    return (mu / 2) * proximal

# === SCAFFOLD: control variates (simplified) ===
class ScaffoldClient(FederatedClient):
    def __init__(self, model, data, client_id):
        super().__init__(model, data, client_id)
        # Local control variate
        self.c_local = {k: torch.zeros_like(v)
                       for k, v in model.state_dict().items()}
    
    def local_train(self, global_weights, global_c, epochs=5, lr=0.01):
        self.model.load_state_dict(global_weights)
        self.model.train()
        
        X, y = self.data
        optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(self.model.parameters(), lr=lr)
        
        for _ in range(epochs):
            idx = np.random.randint(len(X), size=32)
            x_batch = torch.tensor(X[idx], dtype=torch.float32)
            y_batch = torch.tensor(y[idx], dtype=torch.long)
            
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss = F.cross_entropy(self.model(x_batch), y_batch)
            loss.backward()
            
            # Correct gradient: g_i - c_i + c
            for (name, param), c_name in zip(self.model.named_parameters(),
                                              global_c.keys()):
                if param.grad is not None:
                    param.grad -= self.c_local[name] - global_c[name]
            
            optimizer.step()
        
        # Update local control variate
        new_weights = self.model.state_dict()
        for key in self.c_local:
            delta = -(new_weights[key] - global_weights[key]) / (epochs * lr)
            self.c_local[key] -= global_c[key] - self.c_local[key]
            self.c_local[key] += torch.where(
                new_weights[key] - global_weights[key] != 0,
                delta,
                torch.zeros_like(delta)
            )
        
        return new_weights, self.n, deepcopy(self.c_local)

# === Personalized FL: local fine-tuning ===
def personalized_fl(global_model, client_data, ft_epochs=10, lr=0.001):
    """
    Train global model via FedAvg, then fine-tune on each client.
    Each client gets its own personalized model.
    """
    # Global training via FedAvg (omitted)
    fedavg_rounds(global_model, clients)
    
    # Personalize per client
    personal_models = {}
    for client_id, (X, y) in client_data.items():
        personal_model = deepcopy(global_model)
        optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(personal_model.parameters(), lr=lr)
        
        for _ in range(ft_epochs):
            x_tensor = torch.tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32)
            y_tensor = torch.tensor(y, dtype=torch.long)
            
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss = F.cross_entropy(personal_model(x_tensor), y_tensor)
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
        
        personal_models[client_id] = personal_model
    
    return personal_models

# === Gradient compression: top-k sparsification ===
def topk_compress(gradients, sparsity=0.01):
    """Keep only top-k% of gradient values by magnitude."""
    compressed = {}
    for name, grad in gradients.items():
        k = max(1, int(grad.numel() * sparsity))
        values, indices = torch.topk(grad.abs().view(-1), k)
        
        # Store only indices and values
        mask = grad.abs() > values[-1]
        compressed[name] = {
            'values': grad[mask],
            'indices': mask.nonzero().squeeze()
        }
    return compressed

# === q-FedAvg: weighting by inverse of local update magnitude ===
# Clients with large local updates get lower weight in aggregation
# (they likely overfit to their local non-IID distribution)
